result: I keep thinking about the hour just before the world wakes up, when the sky is a muted bruise and the only sound is the neighbor’s cat padding across dew-dusted cedar. It feels like the planet has pressed a collective snooze button, and for a few breaths I am allowed to exist without credentials, notifications, or the small hum of guilt that usually trails me like a second shadow. In that hollow of time I brew coffee so strong it could polish silver, and I listen for the faint percussion of my own heart—an arrhythmic drummer who insists on solos. I imagine the beans traveling from a hillside I will never see, sun sliding down the slopes of Ethiopia or Guatemala the way worries slide off this borrowed quiet. Steam writes ghosts on the kitchen window; I sign my name in the condensation, then.erase it, practicing non-attachment with dish soap and a rough cloth. Somewhere a freight moans, steel wheels translating distance into minor chords. I think of all the letters I never mailed, sealed with the glue of cowardice, stacked like firewood in the drawer. If I struck a match I could warm the whole house with apologies. Instead I pour the coffee into the cup my mother cracked years ago, a hairline fracture that never grew into divorce, just hovered—an unresolved sentence. I drink, I breathe, I wait for the sky to blush like someone who just remembered they are in love. When the first lawn mower coughs, I re-enter the costume of daylight, already nostalgic for the anonymity of almost morning.
